summary.notes: Bootimus recovered via browser-visible page after web_extract failed.

items.name: Bootimus
items.source_story_title: Bootimus – A Self-Contained PXE and HTTP Boot Server
items.summary: Self-contained PXE and HTTP boot server for homelab provisioning.
items.why_included: Commenters called it a killer homelab tool and wanted custom ISO and Firecracker boot flows.
items.evidence: A commenter compared it to pxehost and said they want a good ISO to set up new hosts, plus custom ISO and Kairos/Talos-style tooling.

items.name: pxehost
items.source_story_title: Bootimus – A Self-Contained PXE and HTTP Boot Server
items.summary: Cross-platform PXE host binary for netboot.xyz and local bootstrapping.
items.why_included: A commenter framed it as their less-featureful alternative to Bootimus and used it to solve Docker/ARM network issues.
items.evidence: The comment explicitly says “Last year I released my version of this: https://pxehost.com” and notes Bootimus solved UDP broadcast on ARM macOS better.

items.name: StartupWiki
items.source_story_title: Show HN: StartupWiki – A Free Alternative to Crunchbase
items.summary: Free startup database with profiles, search, and filtering.
items.why_included: Commenters pushed on provenance and verified links, which led the author to add source-citing and agent-ledger features.
items.evidence: The thread centers on source citations, verified badges, and incomplete coverage; the author says verified items should link back to sources and that an agent ledger is now visible.

items.name: CSSQuake
items.summary: Quake recreation rendered with CSS and powered by JavaScript/TypeScript game logic.
items.why_included: Commenters loved the hack and spent the thread comparing browser performance and rough edges.
items.evidence: The discussion repeatedly clarifies CSS is for rendering only, while the game logic is JS/TypeScript, and users report Safari/WebKit versus Chrome/Firefox differences.

items.name: Ember
items.source_story_title: Ember, a native iOS Hacker News reader I built around accessibility
items.summary: Native SwiftUI iOS Hacker News reader built around accessibility.
items.why_included: Commenters praised the accessibility-first design and native comment rendering.
items.evidence: The thread highlights VoiceOver, Dynamic Type, color-blind mode, and native text comments; several commenters asked about App Store plans and usability on mobile.

items.name: UHF X11
items.source_story_title: UHF X11: X11 Built for VisionOS and Apple Vision Pro
items.summary: X11 environment for VisionOS and Apple Vision Pro.
items.why_included: Commenters immediately compared it with Linux headset alternatives like WayVR and discussed GLX compatibility.
items.evidence: One comment points to WayVR as a Linux alternative, while the thread discusses X11 nostalgia, OpenGL/GLX, and what works on Vision Pro.

items.name: make-look-scanned
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Make PDFs look scanned (CLI or in the browser via WASM)
items.summary: CLI and WASM tool that makes PDFs look scanned.
items.why_included: Commenters immediately connected it to print-sign-scan bureaucracy and realism tuning.
items.evidence: The thread discusses signed PDFs that still “had to be scanned,” ImageMagick alternatives, and requests for blank pages and other authenticity tweaks.

items.name: PostgresBench
items.source_story_title: PostgresBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Postgres Services
items.summary: Reproducible benchmark for Postgres services across clouds and deployments.
items.why_included: Commenters wanted cross-cloud comparisons and a way to tune CNPG on Kubernetes.
items.evidence: The comments ask for provider-to-provider comparisons, ask whether it can benchmark CNPG on k8s, and note it takes about 15 minutes to run.

items.name: Tiny
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Tiny – An interpeted dynamic langauge with inline Go native functions
items.summary: Interpreted dynamic language with inline Go native functions.
items.why_included: Commenters framed it as a fit for small tools that still want Go-like deployment.
items.evidence: The author says the niche is CLI apps, automation tools, webview desktop apps, and small HTTP servers shipped as one executable without runtime management.

items.name: F-15 Strike Eagle II reversing project
items.source_story_title: DOS Game "F-15 Strike Eagle II" reversing project needs DOS test pilots
items.summary: DOS-game reverse-engineering project working toward a playable source-reconstructed F-15 Strike Eagle II.
items.why_included: Commenters were excited about a playable DOS release and the long-term mod/port path.
items.evidence: The thread asks for DOSBox or real-DOS testing on version 451.03 and explains the goal is to rebuild the game into modifiable source rather than just emulate it.
